title: Variations on Fefferman's Ball Multiplier Theorem
speaker: Mikael De La Salle
abstract: What happens to an Lp function when one truncates its Fourier transform to a domain? This question is now rather well understood, thanks to famous results by Marcel Riesz and Charles Fefferman, and the answer depends on the domain: if it is a polytope, the function remains Lp integrable. But not necessarily when the domain is curved, such as a Euclidean ball. The lecture will be devoted to another question of the same flavour: What happens to a matrix and its singular values when some of its entries are set to zero?

proof_stages.label: Classical Fourier multiplier benchmarks
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the analogy between Parseval identities for functions and matrices, then recalls Riesz's one-dimensional convergence theorem, Fejer-type smooth summation, and Fefferman's higher-dimensional ball multiplier obstruction as the classical model phenomenon.

proof_stages.label: Noncommutative Lp reconstruction problem for groups
proof_stages.summary: The abelian Fourier-series question is reformulated for a discrete group Gamma using the noncommutative Lp-space of its group von Neumann algebra, asking whether finite-support Fourier multipliers can approximate every element in Lp.

proof_stages.label: Higher-rank evidence and compact spherical inequality
proof_stages.summary: Operator-valued negative results for higher-rank lattices motivate the critical exponent p>2+2/(d-1), whose analytic core is a compact spherical multiplier inequality on SO(d+1)/SO(d).

proof_stages.label: Indicator Fourier multipliers on Lie groups
proof_stages.summary: For a simply connected Lie group G and a C1 domain Omega, the new theorem characterizes Lp-boundedness of the indicator multiplier 1_Omega by local orderability/actions on the real line, equivalently by homomorphisms to the translation, affine, or projective line-action groups.

proof_stages.label: Schur multiplier reduction and classification
proof_stages.summary: The proof is shifted to Schatten-class Schur multipliers: after defining entrywise multipliers and the triangular projection model, a C1 transversal domain Omega in X times X has bounded indicator Schur multiplier exactly when locally Omega is of the form f(x)>g(y).

proof_stages.label: Transfer back and Fefferman-Kakeya obstruction
proof_stages.summary: A dictionary between group and Schur multipliers turns the Schur non-curvature condition into a Lie-algebraic coset condition, while the hard implication is proved by reducing to Meyer's lemma and Fefferman's Euclidean Kakeya counterexample.

examples.name: Free-group radial truncations
examples.note: The free group, viewed through its Cayley tree, is used as a nonamenable positive case where radial finite-support Fourier multipliers can still approximate in noncommutative Lp, showing that amenability is not the whole story.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explains the concrete free-group/tree truncation scheme by measuring word/tree radius, using a radial symbol, and then truncating it

passes.reason: Length-radial finite-support multipliers on the regular free-group tree are symmetric positive approximation tools, not failure cases.

examples.name: SL_n(Z) as a higher-rank lattice test case
examples.note: In response to a question about which groups matter, the speaker points to arithmetic groups such as SL_n(Z) as expected counterexamples to the reconstruction property, illustrating why the higher-rank/operator-algebra motivation is important.
examples.engagement_reason: SL_n(Z) is invoked as a conjectural higher-rank lattice counterexample/test case and as motivation, without a concrete computation on it in the transcript

examples.name: Positive-entry domain in SO(d+1)
examples.note: Taking the multiplier given by the indicator of {g: g_{11}>0} is presented as the compact-group analogue of Fefferman's ball multiplier and leads directly to the broader classification of bounded indicator multipliers.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the classification theorem to the domain where the SO(d+1) matrix entry is positive and concludes non-boundedness from the lack of a relevant action/quotient

passes.reason: The domain {g:g_{11}>0} in SO(d+1) is a K-bi-invariant hemispherical set whose indicator multiplier gives the negative answer.

examples.name: Radial multiplier continuity question in R^2
examples.note: As a Euclidean analogue of the small-p issue, the speaker asks whether an Lp-bounded radial multiplier on R^2 must be continuous away from the origin, showing what the present jump-discontinuity theorem does and does not settle.
examples.engagement_reason: the radial multiplier continuity problem in R^2 is formulated as an open question but no example is analyzed or computed

passes.reason: The R^2 multiplier is assumed radial, hence rotationally symmetric, and is posed as an open continuity question rather than a failure example.

examples.note: In Q&A the indicator of a ball in the regular tree model of F_2 is cited as an open non-Euclidean analogue, known to fail for p>3 but unclear for 2<p<3, illustrating limits of the Euclidean Kakeya reduction.
